result:
I keep thinking about the moment a story becomes *true*. Not factually true—there’s a different department for that—but emotionally true, the instant it stops being ink on a page or vibrations in the air and turns into a small, private country inside whoever’s receiving it. I can’t decide where that border is. Sometimes it feels like a single sentence landing at the exact angle of a reader’s unsaid ache. Other times it’s slower, a cumulative weather system building inside them, sentence by sentence, until the pressure changes and the internal sky cracks open.

I used to believe the author controlled that transition, the way a stage magician palms the coin. Now I’m convinced the reader is the one smuggling it across the checkpoint. The text is just a damaged map; the reader hikes the actual terrain with their own weather in their backpack. Two people can read identical words and one gets a paper cut, the other gets a scar they’ll finger for decades. Same knife, different blood.

There’s a corollary grief in this: once the story moves in, the author is evicted from the reader’s version of it. I can stand on my porch and shout, “No, the blue door means innocence, not escape!” but the reader has already painted it red and installed new hinges. That repainting happens in a room I can’t enter, decorated with furniture I’ve never seen. My sentences become ghosts that haunt other people’s houses; they rearrange the chairs at night and never explain why.

I find that beautiful and a little frightening, like learning your bloodstream has been quietly hosting a conversation among strangers who will never meet. Every book is a transfusion, every reader a hidden donor. We walk around full of borrowed platelets, unaware that our emotional chemistry has been altered by someone else’s private alchemy.

The quiet part of me wants to hoard meaning, to plant a flag and declare, “This is what I meant, nothing more.” The louder part—the part that learned to read by flashlight under blankets—whispers back: *Let it go. Let them keep it. Let it grow teeth or wings in the dark.* Stories aren’t messages in bottles; they’re seeds in digestive systems. They need someone else’s acid to germinate.

So I write, not to transmit certainty, but to mail a package of controlled damage: here are thirty matches, do what you must. Light a candle, burn a bridge, ignite a constellation. I’ll never know. The parcel leaves my hands and instantly becomes Schrodinger’s story—simultaneously alive and dead, on fire and frozen—until a stranger’s pulse collapses the waveform. That collapse is the truest thing we’ll ever share, even though neither of us can point to exactly where or when it happened.

Sometimes, late at night, I imagine all those unwitnessed collapses happening at once, a silent worldwide applause that sounds—if you press your ear to the ground—like a million books closing at slightly different speeds. A soft, papery rainfall of meaning settling over the planet while we sleep, none of us the wiser, all of us quietly remade.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
